question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user craft requests with semantically identical but syntactically different `req.originalUrl` values (e.g., varying query parameter order, URL encoding, or case) to generate distinct MD5 hashes, causing every request to miss the cache and forcing full backend processing for each unique URL variant, increasing node CPU and DB consumption by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user append arbitrary but valid-looking query parameters (e.g., `?limit=25&order=asc&_=<unique_token>`) to cacheable endpoints so that `crypto.createHash('md5').update(req.originalUrl)` produces a unique key per request, defeating the cache entirely and forcing repeated expensive database queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `cacheKeyGenerator` uses the raw `req.originalUrl` without normalization to send requests with percent-encoded characters (e.g., `%61%70%69` vs `api`) that decode to the same path but hash differently, causing cache misses and amplifying backend load by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with extremely long `req.originalUrl` values (e.g., thousands of characters of valid query parameters) to force `crypto.createHash('md5').update(req.originalUrl)` to process large inputs on every request, increasing CPU consumption on the mirror node by over 30% without triggering rate limits?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user craft requests that always result in a cache miss by varying `req.originalUrl` slightly (e.g., adding a unique fragment or unused query param), causing `getCache().getSingleWithTtl(responseCacheKey)` to always return `undefined` and forcing the full request pipeline including DB queries to execute on every request, increasing node resource consumption by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `if-none-match` conditional header (`CONDITIONAL_HEADER`) by sending requests with a crafted ETag value that never matches `cachedResponse.headers[ETAG_HEADER]`, forcing the server to always return a full 200 response body from cache and increasing bandwidth and serialization CPU load by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with a valid `if-none-match` header matching a cached ETag to trigger the 304 path, then immediately re-request the same resource without the header to force full body serialization, alternating between the two patterns to maximize both Redis lookups and response body processing, increasing node resource consumption by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with a crafted `accept-encoding` header that causes `new Negotiator(req)` to perform expensive negotiation logic on every cache hit, and by cycling through many encoding variants (e.g., `gzip;q=0.9, deflate;q=0.8, br;q=0.7, identity;q=0.6`) increase CPU usage in the `negotiate()` function by over 30% compared to baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests without an `accept-encoding: gzip` header to force `negotiate()` to return `false`, causing `cachedResponse.getUncompressedBody()` to be called which invokes `unzipSync()` on every cache hit for compressed responses, increasing CPU decompression load by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user deliberately omit or set `accept-encoding: identity` to force the server to call `cachedResponse.getUncompressedBody()` which calls `unzipSync(Buffer.from(this.body, 'base64url'))` on every single cache hit for compressed entries, causing sustained CPU spikes from repeated decompression operations that increase node resource consumption by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getCacheControlExpiryOrDefault()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `DEFAULT_REDIS_EXPIRY = 1` second fallback in `getCacheControlExpiryOrDefault()` by targeting endpoints that do not set a `cache-control` header, causing all cached responses to expire after 1 second and forcing continuous re-computation of expensive queries, increasing node resource consumption by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: shouldCompress()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly request endpoints that return responses at or just above `config.cache.response.compressThreshold` to ensure `gzipSync(body)` is called on every cache miss response, causing sustained CPU load from synchronous compression operations that increase node resource consumption by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with query parameters in different orders (e.g., `?a=1&b=2` vs `?b=2&a=1`) that are semantically equivalent but produce different MD5 hashes in `cacheKeyGenerator()`, causing cache fragmentation where the same logical resource is stored multiple times in Redis and every variant misses the cache, increasing both Redis memory pressure and backend DB load by over 30%?
